agent_output:
## Executive Summary

This is a textbook CQRS case. The 30-60s query times stem from forcing analytical read patterns (aggregations, rankings, cross-zone stats) through a write-optimized normalized schema (12-table joins across 50M rows). With only ~500 writes/day, the read/write ratio is astronomically skewed toward reads, making CQRS not just appropriate but the *correct* architectural response. I recommend a **lightweight CQRS** approach: keep the existing write model, introduce pre-materialized read models (denormalized projections), and synchronize via domain events вҖ” no separate database engine required initially.

---

## Architecture Proposal

### Components

| Component | Responsibility | Boundaries |
|---|---|---|
| **Order Write Model** | Accepts new orders, status updates, delivery confirmations. Enforces all business invariants (valid transitions, zone assignment, courier capacity). | Owns the normalized schema (12 tables). Does NOT serve dashboard queries. |
| **Event Publisher** | Emits domain events when write model state changes (OrderCreated, StatusChanged, DeliveryCompleted, CourierAssigned). | Owns event schema and delivery guarantees. Does NOT transform data. |
| **Read Model Projector** | Consumes domain events and updates denormalized read models (projection tables). Each projector handles one dashboard concern. | Owns projection logic and read table schemas. Does NOT enforce business rules. |
| **Dashboard Query Service** | Serves the operations dashboard via simple, fast queries against denormalized read tables. | Owns query API. Does NOT write to any table. Read-only. |
| **Projection Rebuild Service** | Replays events or re-queries write model to rebuild read projections from scratch when schema changes or data drifts. | Owns rebuild orchestration. Runs offline/async. |

### Interfaces

| From | To | Contract | Coupling |
|---|---|---|---|
| Order Write Model вҶ’ Event Publisher | Domain events: `{event_type, aggregate_id, timestamp, payload}` | Async, loose вҖ” write model fires and forgets via transactional outbox |
| Event Publisher вҶ’ Read Model Projector | Event stream (ordered per aggregate) | Async, loose вҖ” projector consumes at its own pace |
| Dashboard Query Service вҶ’ Read Tables | Direct SQL against denormalized tables | Sync, tight to read schema (acceptable вҖ” same bounded context) |
| Ops Dashboard (UI) вҶ’ Dashboard Query Service | REST/GraphQL: `GET /dashboard/orders-by-status`, `GET /dashboard/transit-times?zone=X` | Sync, loose вҖ” versioned API |

### Data Flow

```
[New Order / Status Update]
        в”Ӯ
        в–ј
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ    Transactional     в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ  Order Write     в”Ӯв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ Outbox в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–¶в”Ӯ  Event        в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  Model (12 tbls) в”Ӯ    (same DB tx)      в”Ӯ  Publisher    в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ                       в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

                              в–ј                  в–ј                  в–ј
                     в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
                     в”Ӯ  Status       в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  Transit Time в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  Courier      в”Ӯ

                                               в–ј
                                    в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
                                    в”Ӯ  Dashboard Query    в”Ӯ
                                    в”Ӯ  Service            в”Ӯ
                                    в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
                                               в–ј
                                    в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
                                    в”Ӯ  Ops Dashboard UI   в”Ӯ
                                    в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```

### Patterns Used

| Pattern | Rationale |
|---|---|
| **CQRS (lightweight)** | Decouples read optimization from write-model integrity. The extreme read/write asymmetry (dashboard reads vs 500 writes/day) makes this the natural solution. |
| **Transactional Outbox** | Guarantees at-least-once event delivery without distributed transactions. Events are written to an outbox table in the same DB transaction as the write, then published asynchronously. Critical for avoiding the dual-write problem. |
| **Materialized Projections** | Pre-computed, denormalized read tables eliminate the 12-table joins at query time. Each projection is purpose-built for a specific dashboard view. |
| **Event-Driven Projection** | Projectors subscribe to domain events and incrementally update read models. Avoids full re-computation on every change. |

---

## Domain Model

### Bounded Contexts

| Context | Classification | Aggregates |
|---|---|---|
| **Order Management** (write side) | Core | Order, Delivery, CourierAssignment |
| **Operations Reporting** (read side) | Supporting | (no aggregates вҖ” projection tables only) |
| **Courier Management** | Supporting | Courier, CourierSchedule |

### Context Map

| Upstream | Downstream | Relationship |
|---|---|---|
| Order Management | Operations Reporting | **Published Language** вҖ” Order Management publishes well-defined domain events; Reporting consumes them via an ACL that translates events into projection updates. Reporting never queries the write model directly. |
| Courier Management | Order Management | **Customer-Supplier** вҖ” Order Management depends on courier data but doesn't own it. |

---

## Read Model Design (The Core of the Solution)

### Projection 1: Orders by Status

```sql
CREATE TABLE read_orders_by_status (
    status          VARCHAR(32) PRIMARY KEY,
    order_count     BIGINT,
    last_updated    TIMESTAMP
);
-- Query: SELECT * FROM read_orders_by_status;
-- Response time: <5ms
```

**Projector logic:** On `OrderCreated` вҶ’ increment new status count. On `StatusChanged` вҶ’ decrement old, increment new.

### Projection 2: Delivery Success Rates

```sql
CREATE TABLE read_delivery_success_rates (
    zone_id         VARCHAR(64),
    period          DATE,          -- daily granularity
    total_deliveries BIGINT,
    successful      BIGINT,
    failed          BIGINT,
    success_rate    DECIMAL(5,4),  -- pre-computed
    PRIMARY KEY (zone_id, period)
);
-- Query: SELECT * FROM read_delivery_success_rates WHERE period >= NOW() - INTERVAL '30 days';
-- Response time: <10ms
```

### Projection 3: Average Transit Time by Zone

```sql
CREATE TABLE read_transit_time_by_zone (
    zone_id             VARCHAR(64) PRIMARY KEY,
    avg_transit_hours   DECIMAL(8,2),
    p50_transit_hours   DECIMAL(8,2),
    p95_transit_hours   DECIMAL(8,2),
    sample_count        BIGINT,
    last_updated        TIMESTAMP
);
-- Incrementally updated using Welford's online algorithm for running averages
-- Response time: <5ms
```

### Projection 4: Courier Performance Rankings

```sql
CREATE TABLE read_courier_rankings (
    courier_id          VARCHAR(64) PRIMARY KEY,
    courier_name        VARCHAR(128),
    deliveries_completed BIGINT,
    on_time_rate        DECIMAL(5,4),
    avg_delivery_time   DECIMAL(8,2),
    ranking_score       DECIMAL(8,4),  -- composite score, pre-computed
    rank_position       INTEGER,
    last_updated        TIMESTAMP
);
-- Query: SELECT * FROM read_courier_rankings ORDER BY rank_position LIMIT 50;
-- Response time: <5ms
```

**Ranking recalculation:** Triggered on `DeliveryCompleted` events. Re-rank is O(n) on couriers (likely hundreds, not millions) вҖ” fast enough to do inline.

---

## Trade-offs

| Decision | Gains | Sacrifices | Alternatives Considered |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CQRS with same database** | Simple infrastructure (no new DB to operate), atomic outbox writes, team can use existing SQL skills | Read tables share resources with write DB; can't independently scale read side | Separate read DB (Elasticsearch, ClickHouse) вҖ” rejected because 500 writes/day doesn't justify operational overhead of a second data store |
| **Transactional outbox over CDC** | No infrastructure dependency on Debezium/CDC tooling; works with any SQL database; easier to reason about | Polling-based publishing adds slight latency (1-5s typical); outbox table needs cleanup | Change Data Capture (Debezium) вҖ” viable but overkill at this write volume; adds significant operational complexity |
| **Incremental projections over batch refresh** | Near-real-time dashboard updates; constant-time per event | More complex projector code; drift risk if events are lost | Scheduled batch jobs (e.g., refresh every 5 minutes via SQL) вҖ” actually viable at 500 writes/day; discussed below as a stepping stone |
| **Pre-computed aggregates over query-time computation** | Sub-10ms dashboard queries; DB load drops to near-zero for reads | Eventual consistency (seconds, not minutes); projection schema must evolve when dashboard requirements change | Materialized views (DB-native) вҖ” simpler but less flexible; can't easily do cross-table projections or custom ranking logic |

---

## Flexibility Points

| Area | Evolution Path |
|---|---|
| **Read store technology** | Currently same DB. If read load grows (more dashboards, more users), read tables can migrate to a separate replica, or to a purpose-built store (ClickHouse for analytics, Elasticsearch for search) вҖ” projectors just write to a different target. |
| **New dashboard views** | Adding a new projection is additive: write a new projector, create a new read table, replay existing events to backfill. Zero impact on write model. |
| **Event schema** | Events are versioned. New fields added as optional; consumers use schema-on-read. Breaking changes handled via event upcasting. |
| **Projection rebuild** | Any projection can be rebuilt from scratch by replaying events (or by running a one-time query against the write model). This is the escape hatch for bugs or schema changes. |

### Rigidity Points

| Area | Reason |
|---|---|
| **Write model normalization** | The 12-table normalized schema is correct for write integrity. Do NOT denormalize it. The temptation to "just add some indexes" is a trap вҖ” it conflates read and write concerns. |
| **Event contract** | Once published, event schemas are immutable for that version. Downstream projectors depend on them. Changing an event type requires a new version. |
| **Aggregate boundaries** | Order is the aggregate root. All status transitions, delivery confirmations, and courier assignments go through the Order aggregate to protect invariants. |

---

## Risks

| Risk | Severity | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Projection drift** вҖ” read models diverge from write model due to missed events or projector bugs | High | Implement a periodic reconciliation job that compares projection counts against write model counts. Provide a one-click rebuild command per projection. Log every event consumption with idempotency keys. |
| **Outbox table growth** вҖ” outbox accumulates rows if publisher falls behind | Medium | Outbox cleaner job deletes published events after 7-day retention. Alert if outbox depth exceeds 1000 rows (at 500 writes/day, this means >2 days of backlog). |
| **Eventual consistency confusion** вҖ” dashboard users see stale data and file bug reports | Medium | Display "last updated" timestamp on every dashboard widget. Set expectation: data is fresh within 5 seconds. This is a UX problem, not an architecture problem. |
| **Over-engineering** вҖ” CQRS adds complexity that may not be needed if simpler solutions work | Medium | Mitigated by choosing lightweight CQRS (same DB, no event sourcing, no message broker). If even this is too much, start with materialized views as a stepping stone вҖ” see phased approach below. |
| **Initial backfill** вҖ” populating read models from 50M existing records | Low | One-time migration script that queries write model in batches and populates projections. Run during maintenance window. Estimated time: minutes, not hours (projections are small aggregate tables). |

---

## Implementation Guidance

### Recommended Approach

**Phase 1 (Quick Win вҖ” Week 1):** Create the four denormalized read tables and populate them via a batch SQL migration from the existing 50M records. Point the dashboard at these tables. This alone will drop query times from 30-60s to <50ms. Use a scheduled job (every 60 seconds) to refresh the projections. At 500 writes/day, this is perfectly adequate.

**Phase 2 (Event-Driven вҖ” Week 3-4):** Introduce the transactional outbox and event-driven projectors. Replace the scheduled refresh with incremental updates. This gives near-real-time dashboards and sets the foundation for future read models.

**Phase 3 (Optional вҖ” When Needed):** If dashboard load or write volume grows 10x+, move read tables to a separate database or specialized store. The projector architecture makes this a configuration change, not a redesign.

### Files to Create or Modify

- `src/events/domain_events.py` вҖ” Define OrderCreated, StatusChanged, DeliveryCompleted, CourierAssigned event schemas
- `src/write/outbox.py` вҖ” Transactional outbox: write events in same transaction as state changes
- `src/write/outbox_publisher.py` вҖ” Polls outbox table, publishes to projectors, marks as published
- `src/projections/status_projector.py` вҖ” Handles orders-by-status read model
- `src/projections/delivery_rate_projector.py` вҖ” Handles delivery success rate read model
- `src/projections/transit_time_projector.py` вҖ” Handles transit time by zone read model
- `src/projections/courier_ranking_projector.py` вҖ” Handles courier performance read model
- `src/read/dashboard_query_service.py` вҖ” Simple query layer over read tables
- `db/migrations/xxx_create_read_tables.sql` вҖ” Read table schemas
- `db/migrations/xxx_create_outbox_table.sql` вҖ” Outbox table schema
- `db/migrations/xxx_backfill_projections.sql` вҖ” Initial population from write model
- `src/projections/rebuild.py` вҖ” Projection rebuild tool (replay from write model)

### Patterns to Follow

- Every projector must be **idempotent** вҖ” processing the same event twice produces the same result (use event ID deduplication)
- Projectors must handle **out-of-order** events gracefully (use event timestamps, not processing order)
- Read tables should include a `last_updated` timestamp column for observability
- Outbox table schema: `(id, aggregate_type, aggregate_id, event_type, payload JSONB, created_at, published_at)`

### Pitfalls to Avoid

- **Do NOT use event sourcing.** At 500 writes/day with a working write model, event sourcing adds massive complexity for zero benefit. CQRS does not require event sourcing.
- **Do NOT introduce a message broker (Kafka, RabbitMQ) yet.** The transactional outbox with a polling publisher is sufficient at this scale. A broker is warranted at 50,000+ events/day, not 500.
- **Do NOT denormalize the write model.** The temptation to "just flatten a few tables" creates a maintenance nightmare where read concerns leak into write integrity.
- **Do NOT build a generic projection framework.** Four specific projectors are clearer and easier to debug than a generic event-routing engine.
- **Do NOT skip the reconciliation job.** Projection drift is the #1 operational issue with CQRS. Build the safety net from day one.

---

## Confidence Assessment

| | |
|---|---|
| **Confidence Level** | **92%** |
| **High Confidence Areas** | CQRS is the right pattern for this read/write asymmetry; denormalized projections will solve the 30-60s query problem; transactional outbox is the right event delivery mechanism at this scale; phased rollout reduces risk |
| **Low Confidence Areas** | Exact projection schemas depend on dashboard UX requirements not fully specified (e.g., what time periods for success rates? what composite score formula for courier rankings?); whether the existing DB engine can serve both read and write at current and future load without a replica |
| **Assumptions** | The 12-table normalized schema is well-designed for writes and the problem is purely read-path; the team has SQL expertise; no real-time streaming requirements (seconds-fresh is acceptable); the dashboard is internal (operations team), not customer-facing with SLA requirements |

---

### Concerning Anti-Pattern to Watch

The team should resist the gravitational pull toward **"just add indexes and views."** Materialized views in PostgreSQL, for example, require full refresh (no incremental update), lock the table during refresh, and become their own performance problem at 50M rows. They are a band-aid, not a solution. The projection approach gives the same read performance with incremental updates and full control over the refresh logic.

That said вҖ” if the team wants a **zero-code quick win today**, a simple materialized view refreshed every 60 seconds *will* work at 500 writes/day. It's the right stepping stone toward the full CQRS design. Just don't mistake it for the destination.
